name: Usman Khan
title: QA Automation Lead
subject: Re: Usman's regression cycle at Citizens Bank
body:
Hi Usman,

I know my last note wasn't specific enough to be worth your time. Looking at your role leading QA automation at Citizens Bank, I think this angle is a lot more relevant to what you're actually dealing with.

Automation leads at banks tend to face a coverage problem more than a headcount one. Regulatory requirements, new product surfaces, and legacy system updates all keep adding testing scope faster than the team can build against them, and I imagine that keeps your engineers triaging which gaps matter most instead of closing all of them. If I'm reading that wrong, correct me.

It's the same pressure CRED was dealing with. They hit 90% regression coverage running 5x faster on Testsigma, without adding engineers. The shift was plain English test creation, so the whole team could build coverage instead of just the engineers who knew how to code it.

If coverage breadth is the real constraint for your automation team right now, would 15 minutes give you a pretty clear picture of whether Testsigma is worth a look?

Rob Gorham
Testsigma

name: Mehul Savalia
title: QA Lead
subject: Re: Mehul's QA coverage at Citizens Bank
body:
Hi Mehul,

I know my last note wasn't specific enough to be worth your time. Looking at your QA lead role at a financial services company like Citizens Bank, I think this angle is a lot more relevant to what you're actually dealing with.

QA leads at banks spend a meaningful share of each sprint chasing broken tests rather than building new ones. Every UI refresh or backend change upstream can break a chain of existing tests, and I imagine your team is manually patching those failures between release cycles instead of building net-new coverage. If that's not the right read, set me straight.

It's the same maintenance drain MediBuddy was dealing with. They scaled to 2,500 automated tests on Testsigma while cutting maintenance by 50%. The difference was AI self-healing, which stopped locator breakage from turning into a manual repair cycle after every sprint.

If test maintenance is consuming more sprint capacity than your team can afford, would 15 minutes give you a pretty clear picture of whether Testsigma is worth a look?

Rob Gorham
Testsigma

name: Minu Prabhakaran
title: QA Manager
subject: Re: Minu's test maintenance at DISH Network
body:
Hi Minu,

I know my last note wasn't specific enough to be worth your time. Looking at your QA manager role at a streaming and pay-TV company like DISH, I think this angle is a lot more relevant to what you're actually dealing with.

QA managers at media companies usually deal with a platform footprint that keeps expanding. New device types, streaming features, and service integrations all need coverage, and I imagine that means your team is constantly making tradeoffs between keeping up with new features and maintaining what's already in the suite. If I'm off on that, let me know.

It's the same tradeoff Nagra DTV was dealing with. They built 2,500 automated tests in 8 months on Testsigma, running 4x faster, across both UI and API surfaces. The team stopped hand-coding each test case and started scaling coverage without adding engineers.

If that balance between coverage growth and maintenance overhead is a real constraint for your team, would 15 minutes give you a pretty clear picture of whether Testsigma is worth a look?

Rob Gorham
Testsigma

I know my last note wasn't specific enough to be worth your time. Looking at your SQA engineering manager role at a company with DISH's infrastructure footprint, I think this angle is a lot more relevant to what you're actually dealing with.

SQA managers at large media companies tend to deal with a regression suite that gets harder to run quickly as the product surface expands. More devices, more endpoints, more integration points all feed into a regression cycle that's difficult to compress without cutting scope, and I imagine the pressure to release faster only makes that tension harder to manage. If I'm reading that wrong, correct me.

It's the same tension Cisco was dealing with. They cut regression runtime by 35% on Testsigma, without reducing coverage or adding engineers. Parallel execution and automated test repair were what made the cycle shorter instead of the coverage shallower.

If your regression runtime is creating friction on DISH's release cadence, would 15 minutes give you a pretty clear picture of whether Testsigma is worth a look?

name: Dawn McCartha

body:
Hi Dawn,

I know my last note wasn't specific enough to be worth your time. Looking at your role managing testing at a health plan like EmblemHealth, I think this angle is a lot more relevant to what you're actually dealing with.

Testing at a health plan means you can't skip compliance workflows to save time. Member applications, claims, and benefits platforms all have to pass before anything ships, and I imagine that obligation is what keeps the cycle from getting shorter no matter how much pressure there is to release faster. If I'm reading that wrong, set me straight.

It's the same tension Sanofi was dealing with. They got their regression window from 3 days down to 80 minutes on Testsigma, not by cutting what gets tested, but by running those compliance workflows in parallel instead of one at a time. The manual patching work between releases stopped eating into sprint time too.

If that speed-versus-coverage tension is real for your team right now, would 15 minutes give you a pretty clear picture of whether Testsigma is worth a look?
